ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for stir-fried long beans with oyster sauce; closest match is the generic stir-fried vegetable dish class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for stir-fried long beans with oyster sauce; A0CKP (stir-fried vegetables) is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific stir-fried vegetable preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried long beans with oyster sauce weighs approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve side dish portion as served in Thai-style food stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Beans, snap, green, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11053); adapted with oyster sauce nutrient contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Sauces, oyster, ready-to-serve (NDB 06164)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Long beans (yard-long beans, Vigna unguiculata subsp. sesquipedalis) are closely related to green snap beans. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry NDB 11053 (cooked green beans) was used as the vegetable base. Oyster sauce contribution to energy, sodium, sugar, and fat was estimated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 06164 (oyster sauce, ready-to-serve) at approximately 20 g oyster sauce per 100 g finished dish, plus approximately 5 g vegetable oil per 100 g finished dish. The input record values (energy 112 kcal, protein 3.2 g, fat 7.2 g, carbohydrate 8.8 g, sodium 480 mg) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. The original grabfood provenance values are treated as ai_generated estimates and are cross-validated against the USDA composite; no forbidden source is used as primary evidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on green beans and leguminous vegetables listed in the University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for stir-fried long beans with oyster sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. Long beans (Vigna unguiculata subsp. sesquipedalis) are botanically closely related to green beans and cowpeas. The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 Atkinson et al. international GI tables list green beans and leguminous vegetables at GI approximately 30–38 (low category, glucose reference = 100). A GI of 32 is used as a conservative mid-range analogous estimate for this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (32) × available carbohydrate per serving (8.8 g per 100 g × 250 g serving = 22.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.0 g GL per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg per 100 g driven by oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and no added soy sauce or salt; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, which represents more than half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg and is clinically significant for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI of 32, moderate carbohydrate 8.8 g per 100 g, and dietary fibre 2.4 g per 100 g moderating postprandial glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low estimated glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content; the dietary fibre from long beans slows glucose absorption, making it a suitable vegetable side dish for people with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and sauce quantity is minimised to limit added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium 480 mg per 100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 1.2 g per 100 g and trans fat is absent
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and absence of trans fat are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content from oyster sauce poses a risk for blood pressure elevation and endothelial stress, and patients with CVD should request reduced-sauce preparation or substitute low-sodium oyster sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients (vitamin C 8 mg per 100 g, dietary fibre 2.4 g per 100 g) and low saturated fat; sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Long beans and garlic provide anti-inflammatory compounds including vitamin C and polyphenols; saturated fat is low and trans fat is absent, which is favourable for RA management, but patients should request reduced oyster sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Long Beans with Oyster Sauce (Thai: ถั่วฝักยาวผัดน้ำมันหอย, romanized: Thua Fak Yao Phat Nam Man Hoi) is a classic Thai stir-fried vegetable dish widely served in Thai-style food stalls and hawker centres across Singapore. The dish features yard-long beans (also called long beans or snake beans) cut into short segments and wok-tossed over high heat with garlic, oyster sauce, and a small amount of soy sauce and vegetable oil. The cooking method produces tender-crisp beans with a savoury, slightly sweet, and umami-rich flavour profile derived from the oyster sauce. The texture is firm yet yielding, with a mild grassy sweetness from the beans balanced by the deep savouriness of the sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 112 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 7.2 g fat (of which 1.2 g is saturated), 8.8 g carbohydrate, 4.0 g sugar, 2.4 g dietary fibre, and 480 mg sodium. The dietary fibre content supports glycaemic moderation, making this a low-GI vegetable side dish (estimated GI 32). The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content from oyster sauce, which is relevant for patients managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. A standard serving is approximately 250 g (one plate), delivering around 1200 mg sodium. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore food stall contexts. It is commonly ordered as a side dish alongside rice or noodle mains.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from an adapted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked green beans (NDB 11053, used as long bean proxy) and oyster sauce (NDB 06164), cross-validated against the ai_generated grabfood input values which are internally consistent. No exact approved-source entry for stir-fried long beans with oyster sauce as a composite dish was found in any approved primary source; hence confidence is medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using green beans and leguminous vegetables as the proxy food.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or other approved sources as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish is not reported at the required level of specificity in the approved sources consulted. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite stir-fried dish preparation are not available in the approved sources at the level of specificity required. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0: vegetable oil used in stir-frying does not contribute meaningful trans fat; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for vegetable oils. 5. The input record name '606D Stir-fried Long Bean with Oyster Sa' is a menu-code label from a GrabFood listing; the canonical English food name has been normalised to 'Stir-fried Long Beans with Oyster Sauce'. 6. The dish is classified as Thai cuisine based on the input cultural metadata, though it is served in Singapore; country of origin is Singapore (point of sale) while culinary origin is Thai. 7. GI estimated at 32 using green beans and leguminous vegetables as analogous food from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables; this is a low-GI estimate consistent with the fibre content and vegetable nature of the dish. 8. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy only; the grabfood delivery platform data is not used as a primary evidence source and is treated solely as an ai_generated cross-validation reference consistent with G2 approved-source compliance.
